programs.name: Utah Medicaid Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides medical services for low-income seniors and individuals with disabilities, including long-term care options like nursing homes and home care. It covers health care costs for eligible beneficiaries once approved.[2][5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors aged 65+ and people with disabilities meeting income and asset requirements.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by eligibility; covers medical services, nursing home care, and waiver services at no cost to eligible individuals.[5]

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: Not explicitly named in Utah-specific results; federal PACE provides comprehensive medical and social services to help seniors stay in the community. Availability in Utah not detailed in sources.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Nursing home eligible seniors aged 55+ who can live safely in the community with PACE support.
programs.benefit_value: Full coverage of Medicare and Medicaid services including adult day care, transportation, and home care.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: Helps pay Medicare Part A and B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments for eligible low-income seniors. Administered through state programs with income eligibility.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries meeting specific income and resource limits for QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary), SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary), and QI (Qualifying Individual).[5]
programs.benefit_value: Covers premiums and cost-sharing; varies by program (e.g., QMB covers all Medicare costs).[5]

programs.name: SNAP (Food Stamps)
programs.what_it_does: Provides food assistance benefits to purchase groceries for low-income households. Application available through state portals for eligible seniors.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors and disabled individuals meeting federal income guidelines.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Monthly EBT benefits based on household size and income; average $200+ per person for eligible seniors.

programs.name: LIHEAP
programs.what_it_does: Offers energy assistance to help with heating and cooling costs for low-income households. Provides crisis and weatherization-related support through state programs.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors, including those in Utah, Wasatch, and Summit Counties meeting eligibility.[5]
programs.benefit_value: One-time grants for utility bills; amounts vary by need and funding (typically $200-$1,000).

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Improves energy efficiency in low-income homes through insulation, sealing, and other upgrades. Often linked to LIHEAP for comprehensive energy support.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors and disabled households prioritized in Utah programs.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Free home improvements reducing energy costs by 20-30% long-term.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free counseling on Medicare enrollment, prescription drug assistance, and related benefits. Helps screen for federal, state, and local senior benefits.[2][5]
programs.who_its_for: All Utah Medicare beneficiaries, especially low-income seniors in served counties.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling and benefit screening services.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors to support independence and nutrition. Provided through local Area Agencies on Aging and senior centers.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors aged 60+ who are unable to prepare meals due to age or disability.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Daily hot meals delivered; often low or no cost based on income (suggested donation $3-5/meal).

programs.name: Utah Caregiver Support Program (UCSP)
programs.what_it_does: Supports family caregivers with resources, counseling, training, and respite care to delay institutional placement. Includes access to community services and support groups.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Unpaid family caregivers (18+) of seniors 60+, those with Alzheimer's/related disorders, or older relatives (55+) caring for children or disabled adults.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Respite care, counseling, and education; no low-income requirement, services at low or no cost.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides community service and work-based training for older unemployed workers to gain skills and transition to unsubsidized employment. Authorized under Older Americans Act.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income individuals aged 55+ who need training and employment assistance.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Part-time paid training positions at minimum wage, plus support services.

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Offers free legal assistance for issues like benefits, housing, and consumer problems affecting seniors. Provided through Area Agencies on Aging and legal aid societies.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors aged 60+ facing legal barriers to services.[2]

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, investigating complaints and ensuring quality care. Operated through state Aging and Adult Services.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors in nursing homes, assisted living, or other long-term care settings in Utah.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy, complaint resolution, and rights protection services.

programs.name: Utah Aging Waiver
programs.what_it_does: Provides long-term home and community-based services (HCBS) for elderly Utah residents who require nursing home level of care to prevent unnecessary institutionalization. Services include in-home personal care, adult companion services, respite care, home modifications, assistive technology, and personal emergency response systems.[1][2]
programs.who_its_for: Utah residents aged 65 or older requiring nursing home level of care, meeting financial eligibility for Medicaid, though not an entitlement with limited slots and waitlists.[1]
programs.whats_unique: State-specific 1915(c) Medicaid HCBS waiver tailored for seniors 65+, offering a broad array of non-medical supportive services like chore services (snow removal, lawn care) and adult companion services not universally available in other states' waivers; administered statewide with emphasis on delaying nursing home admissions.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by individual care plan and needs; limited enrollment slots, services customized but not cash benefits (e.g., personal care hours, equipment as needed).[1]

programs.name: New Choices Waiver Program
programs.what_it_does: Offers financial assistance and supportive services for long-term care in assisted living or community settings as an alternative to nursing homes. Includes case management, emergency response, respite, personal care, medication management, and non-emergency transportation to reduce out-of-pocket costs.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Utah residents aged 65+, U.S. citizens, permanent residents meeting Medicaid financial limits, who have resided in a nursing home for 90+ days or assisted living for 1 year, needing nursing home level care.[4]
programs.whats_unique: Utah Medicaid waiver specifically enabling transitions from nursing homes to assisted living with targeted services like financial management and specialized equipment; not a standard federal program, unique to state's long-term care strategy.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Package of services to offset assisted living costs; value varies by needs (e.g., attendant care, equipment), aimed at covering long-term care expenses for eligible participants.[4]
